```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fermentation Broth] --> B{Product Location?}
    B -- Extracellular --> C[Solid Liquid Separation]
    B -- Intracellular --> D[Cell Harvest Centrifugation]
    D --> E[Cell Disruption]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Clarified Broth or Cell Lysate]
    F --> G[Precipitation]
    F --> H[Liquid Liquid Extraction]
    F --> I[Aqueous Two Phase Extraction ATPE]
    G --> J[Concentrated Product Stream]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Secondary Purification Chromatography]
```

### 4.2 Centrifugation Decision Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Feed Suspension] --> S2{Particle Size}
    S2 -- 100 micron plus --> S3[Sedimentation Tank]
    S2 -- 1 to 100 micron --> S4[Disk Stack Centrifuge]
    S2 -- 0.1 to 1 micron --> S5[Tubular Bowl Centrifuge]
    S2 -- sub 0.1 micron --> S6[Ultracentrifuge]
    S3 --> S7[Clarified Supernatant]
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
```

### 4.3 Cell Disruption Method Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Cell Pellet] --> M2{Target Type}
    M2 -- Fragile Enzyme --> M3[Enzymatic Lysis Lysozyme]
    M2 -- Recombinant Protein --> M4[High Pressure Homogenizer]
    M2 -- Small Scale Lab --> M5[Sonication]
    M2 -- Bead Mills --> M6[Vibratory Bead Mill]
    M2 -- Gram negative --> M7[Chemical Lysis Detergent]
    M3 --> P1[Cell Lysate]
    M4 --> P1
    M5 --> P1
    M6 --> P1
    M7 --> P1
    P1 --> P2[Centrifugation to Remove Debris]
    P2 --> P3[Supernatant for Downstream]
```

### 4.4 Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Cell Lysate or Broth] --> T2[Add PEG and Salt or Dextran]
    T2 --> T3[Mix and Settle in Mixer Settler]
    T3 --> T4{Phase Separation by Density}
    T4 -- Top Phase PEG Rich --> T5[Target Product Partition]
    T4 -- Bottom Phase Salt Rich --> T6[Contaminants Partition]
    T5 --> T7[Back Extraction or Dialysis]
    T6 --> T8[Waste Stream or Recycle]
```

### 4.5 Multi-Stage Counter-Current Extraction Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Fresh Aqueous Feed] --> XA[Stage 1 Mixer]
    XA --> XB[Stage 1 Settler]
    XB --> XC[Stage 2 Mixer]
    XC --> XD[Stage 2 Settler]
    XD --> XE[Stage n Mixer]
    XE --> XF[Stage n Settler]
    XF --> XR[Final Raffinate]
    XF --> XS[Loaded Organic Solvent Stream]
    XS --> X1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read the diagram:** In counter-current extraction, the organic solvent moves from stage *n → 1* (left in the figure from XF to XA), while the aqueous feed moves *1 → n* (left to right). At equilibrium, the *stage in which the fresh feed enters* receives the most-loaded organic solvent — maximizing the driving force for transfer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define *primary recovery* in downstream processing. List any **four** unit operations classified under primary recovery and justify why they are placed *before* chromatography in the process train.

**Model Answer:**

*Primary recovery* is the set of unit operations in downstream processing whose objective is to **separate the target bio-product from the bulk fermentation broth**, achieving substantial **volume reduction** and **bulk impurity removal** while preserving bioactivity, before high-resolution purification steps are applied.

Four unit operations:

1. **Solid–liquid separation** (centrifugation / filtration) — removes whole cells and debris that would foul chromatography resins.
2. **Cell disruption** (homogenization, sonication, enzymatic lysis) — releases intracellular products into a manageable liquid phase.
3. **Precipitation** (salting-out, isoelectric) — concentrates product and removes gross impurities cheaply.
4. **Liquid–liquid extraction / ATPE** — transfers product into a different phase, reducing volume and removing many contaminants.

*Justification:* These steps handle *large volumes* with *low selectivity*, removing the mass of contaminants that would otherwise clog and overload expensive chromatography columns. Placing them first protects chromatographic media, reduces buffer consumption and overall processing cost.

*(Examiner awarding note: A clear definition earns 1 mark, four operations 1 mark, justification 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

State Stokes' law. What are the conditions under which it is valid? Why does centrifugation use Stokes' law with a modified "effective gravity" rather than a different settling equation?

**Model Answer:**

**Stokes' Law:**

$$v_s = \frac{d_p^{2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

**Validity conditions:**

1. **Reynolds number Re < 0.2** (creeping flow, laminar regime).
2. Particle is **rigid, smooth and spherical**.
3. Fluid is **Newtonian** and **incompressible**.
4. **No particle–particle interaction** (dilute suspension, < 1 % v/v).
5. **No wall effects** (container diameter $\gg d_p$).

**Reason for using modified gravity in centrifugation:** A centrifuge does not change the underlying physics of viscous settling — the same drag force, buoyant force and gravitational force balance applies. Instead, the *radial acceleration* $\omega^{2} r$ replaces $g$, because in a rotating frame the effective "gravity" points outward along the radius. This **substitution** is mathematically rigorous, allowing use of the same Stokes formula with $g \rightarrow \omega^{2} r$, giving:

$$v_{centrifuge} = \frac{d_p^{2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^{2}\,r}{18\,\mu}$$

No *different* equation is needed because the *physical regime* (Re < 0.2) is unchanged — only the driving force changes.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Internal Choice, 2 × 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **cake filtration equation** under constant-pressure conditions, starting from the differential form of Darcy's law. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fermentation broth containing 25 kg·m⁻³ of cell mass is filtered at a constant ΔP = 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa through a filter of area 0.05 m². The medium resistance $R_m = 5 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹ and the specific cake resistance $\alpha = 2 \times 10^{12}$ m·kg⁻¹. The filtrate viscosity $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate:

(i) The slope and intercept of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot.

(ii) The time required to collect 4 L of filtrate.

(iii) The maximum filtration rate at this point. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

Start with Darcy's law:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_m + \alpha c V / A)}$$

Substitute $R_c = \alpha c V/A$:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,R_m + \mu\,\alpha c V / A}$$

Invert and rearrange:

$$\mu R_m\,\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{\mu \alpha c}{A} V \frac{dV}{dt} = A\,\Delta P$$

But more directly, multiply both sides by dt and integrate from $0 \to V$:

$$\int_{0}^{V}\!\!\mu R_m\,dV + \int_{0}^{V}\!\frac{\mu\alpha c}{A}V\,dV = \int_{0}^{t} A\Delta P\,dt$$

$$\mu R_m V + \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2A} V^{2} = A \Delta P\, t$$

Dividing both sides by $V \cdot A \Delta P$:

$$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2}\Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A\Delta P}$$

which is the **linear cake filtration equation**. The plot of $t/V$ versus $V$ is linear with slope $m = \dfrac{\mu\alpha c}{2A^{2}\Delta P}$ and intercept $b = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A\Delta P}$.

*(Award: 3 marks for setup, 2 marks for integration, 2 marks for final form with interpretation.)*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $\Delta P = 1.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa; $A = 0.05$ m²; $c = 25$ kg·m⁻³; $R_m = 5 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹; $\alpha = 2 \times 10^{12}$ m·kg⁻¹; $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**(i) Slope and intercept:**

$$m = \frac{\mu\alpha c}{2A^{2}\Delta P} = \frac{(1.2\times10^{-3})(2\times10^{12})(25)}{2(0.05)^{2}(1.5\times10^{5})}$$

Compute numerator: $(1.2\times10^{-3})(2\times10^{12})(25) = 6.0\times10^{10}$

Compute denominator: $2 \times 2.5\times10^{-3} \times 1.5\times10^{5} = 7.5\times10^{2}$

$$m = \frac{6.0\times10^{10}}{7.5\times10^{2}} = 8.0\times10^{7} \;\; \text{s·m}^{-6}$$

Intercept:

$$b = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} = \frac{(1.2\times10^{-3})(5\times10^{10})}{(0.05)(1.5\times10^{5})}$$

Numerator: $6.0\times10^{7}$; Denominator: $7.5\times10^{3}$

$$b = 8.0\times10^{3} \;\; \text{s·m}^{-3}$$

**(ii) Time to collect $V = 4$ L $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m³:**

$$\frac{t}{V} = mV + b = (8.0\times10^{7})(4\times10^{-3}) + 8.0\times10^{3} = 3.2\times10^{5} + 8.0\times10^{3} = 3.28\times10^{5} \;\; \text{s·m}^{-3}$$

$$t = V \cdot \frac{t}{V} = (4\times10^{-3})(3.28\times10^{5}) = 1312 \;\; \text{s} \approx 21.9 \;\; \text{min}$$

**(iii) Maximum filtration rate** at $V = 4$ L. The instantaneous rate is $dV/dt = (mV + b)^{-2} \cdot A\Delta P/\mu$ — but more simply from the original equation:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \Delta P}{\mu(R_m + \alpha c V/A)}$$

$$R_c = \frac{\alpha c V}{A} = \frac{(2\times10^{12})(25)(4\times10^{-3})}{0.05} = 4.0\times10^{12} \;\; \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_c = 5\times10^{10} + 4\times10^{12} \approx 4.05\times10^{12} \;\; \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{(0.05)(1.5\times10^{5})}{(1.2\times10^{-3})(4.05\times10^{12})} = \frac{7500}{4.86\times10^{9}} = 1.54\times10^{-6} \;\; \text{m}^{3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$= 1.54 \;\; \text{mL·s}^{-1}$$

*(Award: 2 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii) with full unit consistency.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **various methods of cell disruption** for intracellular products. Compare mechanical and non-mechanical methods with respect to **scale-up potential, product suitability and energy requirement**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A recombinant protein is released from *E. coli* using a high-pressure homogenizer. The disruption follows the first-order release model $R = R_{\max}(1 - e^{-kN})$ with $R_{\max} = 0.95$ and $k = 0.42$ per pass.

(i) Calculate the protein release after 2, 4 and 6 passes.

(ii) How many passes are required to achieve **90 %** of $R_{\max}$?

(iii) If the homogenizer processes 1000 L of broth at a pressure drop of 600 bar per pass, calculate the total energy input in kWh. (Take 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa; assume 100 % mechanical-to-fluid work conversion for the purpose of this estimation.) (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Block diagram of cell disruption methods:**

1. **Mechanical methods**
   - High-pressure homogenization (Manton–Gaulin, APV)
   - Bead milling (vibratory or agitator)
   - Sonication (probe / bath)
2. **Non-mechanical methods**
   - Chemical lysis (detergents, chaotropes, alkali)
   - Enzymatic lysis (lysozyme, glucanases)
   - Osmotic shock
   - Freeze–thaw
   - Electrical (electroporation)

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Mechanical | Non-Mechanical |
|---|---|---|
| Scale-up potential | Excellent (industrial homogenizers handle > 10 m³·h⁻¹) | Limited (enzymes expensive; detergents create downstream issues) |
| Product suitability | Robust proteins & inclusion bodies | Labile proteins, periplasmic products |
| Energy requirement | High (600–1000 bar, 0.5–5 kWh·m⁻³) | Low (chemical) but reagent cost is high |
| Heat generation | Significant — cooling needed | Mild |
| Continuous operation | Yes | Often batch |

**Conclusion:** Mechanical methods dominate *large-scale industrial DSP*; non-mechanical methods are reserved for *lab-scale* or *specific* products.

*(Award: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for conclusion.)*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:** $R_{\max} = 0.95$, $k = 0.42$ per pass.

**(i) Release at $N$ passes:**

At $N = 2$:
$$R = 0.95\,(1 - e^{-0.42 \times 2}) = 0.95\,(1 - e^{-0.84}) = 0.95\,(1 - 0.4317) = 0.95 \times 0.5683 = 0.540$$

At $N = 4$:
$$R = 0.95\,(1 - e^{-1.68}) = 0.95\,(1 - 0.1864) = 0.95 \times 0.8136 = 0.773$$

At $N = 6$:
$$R = 0.95\,(1 - e^{-2.52}) = 0.95\,(1 - 0.0805) = 0.95 \times 0.9195 = 0.874$$

**(ii) Passes for 90 % of $R_{\max}$:**

$0.9 \times 0.95 = 0.855$, so $R = 0.855$.

$$0.855 = 0.95\,(1 - e^{-0.42 N})$$
$$\frac{0.855}{0.95} = 1 - e^{-0.42 N}$$
$$e^{-0.42 N} = 0.1$$
$$-0.42 N = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$
$$N = \frac{2.3026}{0.42} \approx 5.48 \;\; \text{passes}$$

Rounding up, **$N = 6$ passes** are required (since fractional pass is not physically meaningful).

**(iii) Total energy input:**

Pressure drop per pass = $600$ bar $= 600 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 6 \times 10^{7}$ Pa.

Volume per pass = 1000 L = 1 m³.

Energy per pass (work = pressure × volume):

$$W_{pass} = \Delta P \cdot V = (6\times10^{7})(1) = 6\times10^{7} \;\; \text{J}$$

Total passes $N = 6$ (from part ii):

$$W_{total} = 6 \times 6\times10^{7} = 3.6\times10^{8} \;\; \text{J}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ J):

$$W_{total} = \frac{3.6\times10^{8}}{3.6\times10^{6}} = 100 \;\; \text{kWh}$$

*(Award: 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii) with units and conversions shown.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Stokes' law used without verifying Re < 0.2** → Deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., L → m³, bar → Pa) → Most common reason for *arithmetic marks lost*.
> 3. **Writing the cake filtration equation without deriving it** → Step marks not awarded; only final formula marks.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ (release fraction) with $R_m$ (medium resistance)** in disruption problems.
> 5. **Omitting the *justification*** in the "why primary recovery before chromatography" question — definition alone is not enough.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Primary recovery = volume reduction + bulk impurity removal** — not purification.
- ⭐ **Stokes' law** valid only for **Re < 0.2**, rigid spherical particles, dilute suspension.
- ⭐ **Σ-method** is the standard tool for **scale-up of centrifuges**.
- ⭐ **Cake filtration** gives a **linear $t/V$ vs $V$ plot**; slope and intercept yield $\alpha$ and $R_m$.
- ⭐ **Cell disruption** first-order model: $R = R_{\max}(1 - e^{-kN})$ — common in homogenizer problems.
- ⭐ **Cohn equation** $\log S = \beta - K_s I$ — salting-out constant $K_s$ is positive for *typical* globular proteins.
- ⭐ **Isoelectric precipitation** requires $\text{pH} = \text{pI}$ — minimum solubility.
- ⭐ **Distribution coefficient** $K_D = C_{org}/C_{aq}$; higher $K_D$ → fewer stages needed.
- ⭐ **Counter-current** extraction always outperforms **cross-current** at equal $n$ and $E$.
- ⭐ **Aqueous two-phase extraction (ATPE)** — uses PEG/dextran or PEG/salt systems; biocompatible; ideal for labile biomolecules.
- ⭐ **Energy** for cell disruption scales linearly with **pressure × volume × passes** in kWh.
- ⭐ Always state **boundary conditions** alongside any formula — this is what differentiates a 70 % answer from a 95 % answer in KTU valuation.
- ⭐ **Material balance closure** is mandatory in multi-step recovery calculations; missing it loses 1–2 marks per sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Disruption by Chemical, Mechanical, and Enzymatic Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBTT601 - DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cell Disruption by Chemical, Mechanical, and Enzymatic Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cell Disruption?

**Cell Disruption** (also called *cell lysis* or *cell rupture*) is a unit operation in Downstream Processing (DSP) in which the structurally robust **cell envelope** (cell wall + cell membrane) of a microorganism, plant, or animal cell is deliberately and irreversibly broken so that the intracellular bio-product of interest (recombinant proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids, metabolites, inclusion bodies, pigments, lipids, etc.) can be released into the surrounding aqueous suspension for subsequent recovery and purification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):**
> *"Cell disruption is the process of breaking the cell wall and/or membrane to release intracellular products. The choice of method is governed by the cell type (Gram-positive, Gram-negative, yeast, mammalian, plant), the nature of the product (periplasmic, cytoplasmic, or membrane-bound), product stability (heat, shear, pH sensitivity), scale of operation, and downstream compatibility."*

Mathematically, **Disruption Yield** is defined as the fractional release of soluble intracellular protein:

$$Y_d = \frac{R}{R_{max}}$$

where:
- $Y_d$ = disruption yield (dimensionless, $0 \le Y_d \le 1$)
- $R$ = released soluble protein concentration at time $t$ (mg/L or g/L)
- $R_{max}$ = maximum releasable soluble protein concentration (mg/L or g/L)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Cracking a Walnut":**
> Imagine you want to extract the kernel inside a walnut.
> - **Mechanical disruption** = using a hammer, pliers, or a nutcracker (apply brute physical force).
> - **Chemical disruption** = soaking the walnut in a solvent that dissolves/shrinks the shell (treat with chemicals).
> - **Enzymatic disruption** = letting fungi/bacteria secrete enzymes (like cellulase) that slowly digest the shell (biological degradation).
>
> Each method has trade-offs: the hammer is fast but messy (denatures the kernel), the chemical soak is clean but slow, and the enzyme is gentle but expensive.

## 1.3 Why is Cell Disruption Critical in DSP?

- Over **30–40% of total DSP cost** is attributable to disruption in intracellular product recovery.
- It is a **mandatory prerequisite** when the desired bio-product is *not secreted* extracellularly by the host.
- The choice of method directly governs the **purity profile**, **yield**, and **activity** of the product entering capture chromatography.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

| S.No | Method Class | Governing Principle | Typical Energy Input | Typical Yield (single pass) |
|:----:|:-------------|:--------------------|:--------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Shear, impact, cavitation | 10⁶ – 10⁹ W/m³ | 60 – 95 % |
| 2 | **Chemical** | Solubility / charge alteration | Low (chemical potential) | 40 – 80 % |
| 3 | **Enzymatic** | Bio-catalytic hydrolysis of wall polymers | Very low (thermal) | 70 – 99 % (specific walls) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests the **matching of cell type to disruption method** (e.g., "Lysozyme is used for…?" → Gram-positive bacteria). Memorize the mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Disruption Kinetics (Universal Model)

The release of intracellular protein from cells under mechanical stress follows a **first-order kinetic model** proposed by Hetherington et al. and adopted by KTU examiners as the canonical disruption equation.

**Assumption:** All cells in the population are equally susceptible to disruption.

$$\boxed{\;\ln\left(\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = k_d \cdot t\;}$$

where:
- $R_{max}$ = maximum protein released at infinite time (g/L)
- $R$ = protein released at time $t$ (g/L)
- $k_d$ = first-order disruption rate constant (s⁻¹ or min⁻¹)
- $t$ = time of exposure (s or min)

A more compact form is obtained by exponentiating:

$$R(t) = R_{max}\left[1 - e^{-k_d t}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is **the single most-tested formula** in Module 2. KTU asks derivations of this in Part B (14 marks) almost every semester.

### 2.1.1 Why first order?
For a population $N$ of intact cells:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N \quad \Rightarrow \quad N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-k_d t}$$

Fraction disrupted $= (N_0 - N)/N_0 = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$, which (assuming protein release is proportional to cell breakage) gives the same exponential form.

---

## 2.2 Mechanical Methods — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 High-Pressure Homogenizer

**Operating principle:** Cell suspension is forced under very high pressure (**5,000 – 30,000 psi**, i.e., ~ **35 – 200 MPa**) through a narrow annular orifice. Three disruption mechanisms act simultaneously:

1. **Impingement** on the impact ring (laminar shear + sudden deceleration).
2. **Shear stress** in the orifice.
3. **Cavitation** due to sudden pressure drop downstream.

**Disruption equation (Follows et al.):**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\left(\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = k_p \cdot N_p \cdot P^a\;}$$

where:
- $N_p$ = number of passes through the homogenizer (dimensionless)
- $P$ = operating pressure (bar or psi)
- $a$ = pressure exponent (typically $0.9$ – $1.2$ for *E. coli*, $\approx 1.6$ for yeast)
- $k_p$ = rate constant depending on cell type and temperature

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship is **nearly linear with pressure** ($a \approx 1$) for most bacteria, but **super-linear for yeast** due to their tough chitin-glucan wall.

**Operating envelope:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|:----------|:-------------:|
| Pressure | **15,000 – 25,000 psi** (≈ 100 – 170 MPa) |
| Cell density | 10 – 25 % w/v wet cells |
| Temperature rise | **+1.5 – 2.5 °C per 10,000 psi** |
| Number of passes | 1 – 4 (single pass often sufficient) |
| Yield | **70 – 95 %** |

### 2.2.2 Bead Mill (Bead Mill / Stirred-Bead Mill)

**Principle:** Glass, ceramic, or zirconia beads (diameter **0.1 – 1.0 mm**) are agitated with the cell slurry at high speed. Cells are disrupted by **shear, impact, and attrition** between beads.

**Critical parameters:**
- **Bead loading** ($\phi$): 50 – 90 % v/v of grinding chamber.
- **Bead diameter** ($d_b$): smaller beads = higher disruption but higher viscosity heating.
- **Agitator tip speed** ($v_t$): typically **5 – 15 m/s**.
- **Cell concentration** ($X$): 5 – 20 % w/v wet cells.

**Rate equation:**

$$\boxed{\;k_d \;=\; C_1 \cdot v_t^\beta \cdot d_b^{\gamma} \cdot \phi^{\delta}\;}$$

Empirical exponents (for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*):
- $\beta \approx 1.0$
- $\gamma \approx -0.6$ (smaller beads give higher $k_d$, but only up to a limit)
- $\delta \approx 0.7$

**Specific energy input:**

$$E_s = \frac{P_{motor}}{\dot{m}_{slurry}} \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

Optimal $E_s$ for yeast ≈ **5 – 10 MJ/kg suspension**.

### 2.2.3 French Press

A piston-driven laboratory-scale device that extrudes cell slurry through a narrow orifice at pressures up to **40,000 psi (≈ 275 MPa)**. Mechanistically identical to a homogenizer but at **batch scale (5 – 50 mL)**. Used mainly in research and process development, not commercial production.

### 2.2.4 Ultrasonication (Sonication)

**Principle:** High-frequency sound waves (**20 – 100 kHz**) generate **acoustic cavitation** — microbubbles collapse violently, producing localized shock waves of **> 1000 atm** and temperatures **> 5000 K** in micro-zones.

**Tip probe equation for protein release:**

$$R(t) = R_{max}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-k_{us}\, t\right)\right]$$

where

$$k_{us} = k_0 \left(\frac{P_{ac}}{P_0}\right)^{1.5}$$

$P_{ac}$ = acoustic power, $P_0$ = reference power.

> [!WARNING]
> Sonication generates extreme local heat. Always use an **ice-bath jacket**. Prolonged sonication denatures proteins and fragments nucleic acids (which complicates viscosity).

### 2.2.5 Cryogenic Grinding (Mortar & Pestle / Cryo-Mill)

Cells are flash-frozen in **liquid nitrogen (–196 °C)** and ground with a pre-cooled mortar/pestle or a cryogenic ball mill. The frozen water acts as a brittle matrix, fracturing cell walls on impact. Ideal for **thermolabile intracellular products** and for **recalcitrant plant tissues**.

---

## 2.3 Chemical Methods — Deep Theory

Chemical disruption weakens or dissolves the cell envelope through pH shock, surfactants, chaotropes, or solvents.

### 2.3.1 Alkali Treatment

Strong bases (**NaOH 0.1 – 1.0 M**, pH 11–13) **saponify membrane lipids** and **hydrolyse peptidoglycan**. Effective and cheap for **non-protein products** (e.g., nucleic acids, certain polysaccharides). However, at pH > 11, proteins undergo **deamidation** and **cysteine degradation** — so unsuitable for most recombinant proteins.

**Half-life of a typical protein at pH 12:**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{deam}} \quad \text{where} \quad k_{deam} \propto 10^{(pH - pK_a)}$$

### 2.3.2 Acid Treatment

Acids (**HCl 0.5 – 2.0 M**, pH 1–2) disrupt **outer membranes** and acidify the cytoplasm. Used industrially for **intracellular enzyme extraction from fungal mycelia** (e.g., aspergillopeptidase). Care needed for acid-labile products.

### 2.3.3 Detergents (Surfactants)

Detergents **solubilize the phospholipid bilayer**, releasing membrane-bound and periplasmic proteins.

| Detergent | Class | CMC (mM) | Application |
|:----------|:------|:--------:|:------------|
| **SDS** | Anionic | 8.2 | Strong, denaturing; solubilises nearly all membranes |
| **CTAB** | Cationic | 0.9 | Used for nucleic acid extraction (binds DNA) |
| **Triton X-100** | Non-ionic | 0.2 – 0.9 | Gentle; preserves enzyme activity |
| **CHAPS** | Zwitterionic | 6.0 | Mild, dialysis-removable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Part A (3 marks)**, KTU frequently asks: *"Name two detergents used in cell disruption and state their ionic class."* Memorize the table above.

**Solubilization equation** (for membrane protein release):

$$\frac{R}{R_{max}} = \frac{[D]}{[D] + C_{50}}$$

where $[D]$ = detergent concentration, $C_{50}$ = concentration giving 50 % release.

### 2.3.4 Chaotropes and Denaturants

- **Urea (6 – 8 M)**: disrupts hydrogen-bond network of proteins and loosens membranes.
- **Guanidine HCl (4 – 6 M)**: more powerful chaotrope, fully denaturing.
- **Phenol**: protein denaturant used historically for nucleic acid prep (now replaced).

### 2.3.5 Solvents

- **Toluene, chloroform, acetone, isopropanol** disrupt lipid bilayers.
- Widely used to release **periplasmic enzymes** (e.g., using toluene to release invertase from yeast).
- Toluene is **not permitted** in food/pharma applications.

---

## 2.4 Enzymatic Methods — Deep Theory

Enzymatic lysis uses **lytic enzymes** to hydrolyse specific cell-wall polymers under mild conditions (pH 6–8, 25–37 °C), making it the **gentlest** disruption technique.

### 2.4.1 Lysozyme (for Gram-positive bacteria)

**Mechanism:** Hydrolyses the **β-1,4-glycosidic bond** between *N*-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) and *N*-acetylglucosamine (NAG) of peptidoglycan.

$$(NAG-NAM)_n + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Lysozyme}} (NAG-NAM)_{n-1} + \text{NAG or NAM}$$

**Optimal conditions:**
- pH **6.2 – 7.5** (maximum activity at pH ~ 6.2)
- Temperature **25 – 37 °C**
- Concentration **0.2 – 1.0 mg/mL**
- Pretreatment with **EDTA (1 – 5 mM)** is essential for Gram-negative cells (outer membrane is lipopolysaccharide).

### 2.4.2 Other Lytic Enzymes and Targets

| Enzyme | Cell Target | Bond Hydrolysed |
|:-------|:------------|:----------------|
| **Lysozyme** | Gram-positive bacteria | β-1,4 NAM–NAG |
| **Zymolyase / Glusulase** | Yeast | β-1,3-glucan |
| **Cellulase** | Plant cells | β-1,4-glucan |
| **Pectinase** | Plant cells | α-1,4-pectin |
| **Chitinase** | Fungal cell wall | β-1,4-NAG |
| **Proteinase K** | General | Peptide bonds (often used in lysis buffer) |
| **Achromopeptidase** | Gram-positive & -negative | Peptidoglycan |
| **Mannanase** | Yeast (mannoprotein) | α-mannan |

### 2.4.3 Combined EDTA + Lysozyme (for Gram-negative bacteria)

Gram-negative bacteria are protected by an **outer membrane** rich in LPS, which is stabilized by divalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺). **EDTA chelates** these cations → destabilizes the outer membrane → exposes the underlying peptidoglycan → lysozyme then hydrolyses it.

**Sequential protocol:**

$$\text{EDTA (1-5 mM, 4°C, 10 min)} \rightarrow \text{Lysozyme (0.2 mg/mL, 37°C, 30 min)}$$

### 2.4.4 Phage Lysis (Emerging Technique)

Engineered **bacteriophage endolysins** are being explored for highly specific bacterial lysis (e.g., anti-*Staphylococcus aureus* endolysin). Not yet mainstream but an active research area in 2024–25.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Selection Matrix

| Criterion | Mechanical | Chemical | Enzymatic |
|:----------|:-----------|:---------|:----------|
| **Speed** | Fast (sec – min) | Moderate (min – h) | Slow (h) |
| **Cost** | High capex, low opex | Low capex, high opex | Very high opex |
| **Scale-up** | Excellent | Easy | Difficult |
| **Heat generation** | High | Low | Negligible |
| **Product denaturation risk** | Medium-High | High (extreme pH) | Very Low |
| **Sterility** | Easy (CIP/SIP) | Difficult (chemical residues) | Easy (filter-sterilizable) |
| **Selectivity** | Non-selective | Non-selective | **Highly selective** |
| **Best for** | Bulk intracellular protein | Nucleic acids, pigments | Labile / periplasmic enzymes |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 **HOT question**: *"Justify why enzymatic disruption is preferred for therapeutic proteins despite being expensive."*
> **Model answer:** *Because the mild pH/temperature preserves tertiary structure and biological activity; chemical contamination is avoided; FDA/regulatory pathways are cleaner.*

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all equations** tested in Module 2. Replicate it on a single revision card.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Values |
|:-:|:--------|:--------------|:---------------|
| 1 | $Y_d = R/R_{max}$ | Disruption yield (fractional) | 0 ≤ Y ≤ 1 |
| 2 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = k_d t$ | First-order disruption kinetics | Universal |
| 3 | $R(t) = R_{max}\!\left[1 - e^{-k_d t}\right]$ | Direct form | Universal |
| 4 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$ | Surviving intact cells | Universal |
| 5 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = k_p N_p P^a$ | Homogenizer kinetics | $a \approx 0.9-1.6$ |
| 6 | $k_d = C_1\, v_t^{\beta} d_b^{\gamma} \phi^{\delta}$ | Bead-mill rate | $\beta \approx 1, \gamma \approx -0.6$ |
| 7 | $E_s = P_{motor}/\dot{m}_{slurry}$ | Specific energy input | 5–10 MJ/kg for yeast |
| 8 | $\dfrac{R}{R_{max}} = \dfrac{[D]}{[D] + C_{50}}$ | Detergent solubilization | $C_{50}$ in mM |
| 9 | $\Delta T \approx 2.5 °C$ per 10,000 psi | Homogenizer heating | Empirical |
| 10 | CMC: SDS 8.2 mM, CTAB 0.9 mM, Triton X-100 0.2–0.9 mM | Detergent reference | Critical micelle conc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - At $t \to 0$ : $R = 0$
> - At $t \to \infty$ : $R \to R_{max}$ (asymptotic, never exceeded)
> - For **single-pass** homogenizer, replace $N_p$ by 1.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Disruption Kinetics

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

Let $N(t)$ = number of intact (undisrupted) cells per unit volume at time $t$. Each cell has an equal probability of disruption per unit time, governed by rate constant $k_d$ (units: s⁻¹).

### 3.1.2 Differential Equation

The rate of decrease in intact cell number is proportional to $N(t)$:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N(t)$$

### 3.1.3 Separation of Variables

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \cdot dt$$

### 3.1.4 Integration

Integrating from $t = 0$ (when $N = N_0$) to arbitrary time $t$ (when $N = N(t)$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right] = -k_d t$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for $N(t)$

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-k_d t}$$

The fraction disrupted at time $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{N_0 - N(t)}{N_0} = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$$

### 3.1.6 Relating to Released Protein $R$

If released protein $R$ is proportional to the number of disrupted cells, then $R(t) = R_{max} \cdot f(t)$:

$$R(t) = R_{max}\!\left[1 - e^{-k_d t}\right]$$

### 3.1.7 Rearranging into the KTU Form

Subtract from $R_{max}$:

$$R_{max} - R(t) = R_{max} e^{-k_d t}$$

Divide by $R_{max}$:

$$\frac{R_{max} - R(t)}{R_{max}} = e^{-k_d t}$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max} - R(t)} = e^{+k_d t}$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = k_d \cdot t\;}$$

This is the **canonical KTU equation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Homogenizer Pressure-Exponent Equation

### 3.2.1 Hypothesis

The disruption rate constant $k_d$ for a high-pressure homogenizer depends on operating pressure $P$ through a power law:

$$k_d = k_p \cdot P^{a}$$

### 3.2.2 Substitute into Universal Equation

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right) = (k_p P^{a}) \cdot N_p$$

### 3.2.3 Linearisation for Graphical $k_p$ and $a$

Take logarithm of both sides:

$$\log\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max}-R}\right)\right] = \log k_p + a \cdot \log P + \log N_p$$

A plot of $\log\!\left[\ln(R_{max}/(R_{max}-R))\right]$ versus $\log P$ at constant $N_p$ yields a straight line:
- **Slope** = $a$ (pressure exponent)
- **Intercept** = $\log k_p + \log N_p$

> [!NOTE]
> Experimentally, for *E. coli* K-12: $a \approx 0.96$ and $k_p \approx 7.7 \times 10^{-9}$ (units depend on $P$ in Pa and time in s).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bead-Mill Power-Law Expression

### 3.3.1 Empirical Foundation

Multiple studies (Limon-Lason et al., 1979; Rehacek & Schaefer, 1977) have shown that for stirred bead mills:

$$k_d \propto v_t^{\beta} \cdot d_b^{\gamma} \cdot \phi^{\delta}$$

### 3.3.2 Dimensionless Constants

For *S. cerevisiae* at $30 °C$ in a 5-L Netzsch bead mill:

$$k_d \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \cdot v_t^{1.0} \cdot d_b^{-0.6} \cdot \phi^{0.7}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $v_t = 10$ m/s, $d_b = 0.5$ mm, $\phi = 0.7$, and $t = 5$ min. Calculate $Y_d$.

**Step 1:** Compute $k_d$:

$$k_d = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (10)^{1.0} \cdot (0.5)^{-0.6} \cdot (0.7)^{0.7}$$

Computing each factor:

$$10^{1.0} = 10$$

$$0.5^{-0.6} = \frac{1}{0.5^{0.6}} = \frac{1}{0.6598} \approx 1.516$$

$$0.7^{0.7} \approx 0.779$$

$$k_d \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \times 1.516 \times 0.779$$

$$k_d \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 11.81 \approx 3.54 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Convert $t$ to seconds:

$$t = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply universal equation:

$$Y_d = 1 - e^{-k_d t} = 1 - e^{-(3.54 \times 10^{-3})(300)}$$

$$k_d \cdot t = 1.062$$

$$Y_d = 1 - e^{-1.062} = 1 - 0.346 \approx 0.654$$

**Result:** Disruption yield = **65.4 %** in a single 5-minute pass.

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves such **numerical problems**. Always show the units in every step.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Detergent Solubilization (Langmuir-type)

### 3.4.1 Binding Hypothesis

A detergent molecule $D$ binds a membrane protein $P$ to form a solubilized complex $PD$:

$$P + D \rightleftharpoons PD$$

### 3.4.2 Equilibrium Expression

With dissociation constant $K_D = C_{50}$:

$$K_D = \frac{[P][D]}{[PD]}$$

### 3.4.3 Fraction Solubilized

$$Y_d = \frac{[PD]}{[P]_0} = \frac{[D]}{[D] + K_D} = \frac{[D]}{[D] + C_{50}}$$

### 3.4.4 Limiting Cases

- When $[D] \ll C_{50}$ : $Y_d \approx [D]/C_{50}$ (linear regime)
- When $[D] \gg C_{50}$ : $Y_d \to 1$ (saturation)

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Pattern Bank

### Pattern A — Yield at given time

> **Problem frame:** *A *E. coli* suspension is disrupted in a high-pressure homogenizer. If $k_d = 0.05$ min⁻¹ and $R_{max} = 800$ mg/L, find the concentration of protein released after 20 minutes.*

**Solution:**

$$R(20) = 800 \left[1 - e^{-(0.05)(20)}\right] = 800 \left[1 - e^{-1}\right]$$

$$e^{-1} \approx 0.368$$

$$R(20) = 800 (1 - 0.368) = 800 \times 0.632 = 505.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Pattern B — Time to reach a given yield

> **Problem frame:** *How long does it take to reach 90 % disruption if $k_d = 0.04$ min⁻¹?*

**Solution:**

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-k_d t}$$

$$e^{-k_d t} = 0.1$$

$$k_d t = \ln 10 \approx 2.303$$

$$t = \frac{2.303}{0.04} = 57.6 \text{ min}$$

### Pattern C — Homogenizer pressure effect

> **Problem frame:** *A homogenizer achieves 60 % disruption at 10,000 psi in 1 pass. If the pressure is doubled (and $a = 1.0$), what is the new yield?*

**Solution:**

At $P_1 = 10{,}000$ psi, $\ln(R_{max}/(R_{max}-R_1)) = k_p P_1^{a} = k_p \cdot 10{,}000$.
Let $X_1 = \ln(R_{max}/(R_{max}-R_1))$.

Since $R_1 = 0.6 R_{max}$:

$$X_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{0.4 R_{max}}\right) = \ln(2.5) = 0.916$$

At $P_2 = 2P_1$ and $a = 1$, $X_2 = 2 X_1 = 1.832$.

$$\frac{R_{max}}{R_{max} - R_2} = e^{1.832} = 6.247$$

$$R_{max} - R_2 = \frac{R_{max}}{6.247} = 0.160 R_{max}$$

$$R_2 = 0.840 R_{max} = 84\%$$

**Answer:** Yield increases from **60 % → 84 %** when pressure doubles.

### Pattern D — Bead-mill parameter effect

> **Problem frame:** *If bead diameter is halved (and $\gamma = -0.6$), how does $k_d$ change?*

**Solution:**

$$\frac{k_{d,new}}{k_{d,old}} = \left(\frac{d_{b,new}}{d_{b,old}}\right)^{-0.6} = (0.5)^{-0.6} = 1.516$$

**Answer:** $k_d$ increases by **51.6 %**, i.e., disruption is ~ 1.52× faster.

---
