# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark direct/short-answer questions
> - **Part B:** 14-mark in-depth questions, with internal choice between **Q-A** and **Q-B**
> - **Internal Choice Convention:** Both Q-A and Q-B carry equal marks and belong to the same module; examiner offers the student a choice.

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]
**Define dialysis. What is the role of MWCO in selecting a dialysis membrane?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dialysis is a **membrane-based separation process** in which solute molecules are separated based on their **differential diffusion rates** across a semi-permeable membrane. The driving force is the **concentration gradient** between the feed (retentate) and the receiving buffer (dialysate). **(1 mark)**

The **MWCO (Molecular Weight Cut-Off)** is a membrane specification that defines the molecular weight of the smallest solute that is **90% rejected** by the membrane. **(1 mark)**

In selecting a dialysis membrane, the MWCO is chosen such that:
- **Target macromolecules** (e.g., a protein with MW = 60 kDa) are fully retained (MWCO ≪ 60 kDa, typically one-third the size).
- **Impurities** (e.g., salts MW < 1 kDa) freely pass through. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]
**List any three industrial dryers used in downstream processing. State the principle of freeze drying in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three industrial dryers used in downstream processing:
1. **Spray dryer** – atomizes liquid feed into hot air. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Freeze dryer (lyophilizer)** – removes water by sublimation from frozen state. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Fluidized-bed dryer** – suspends wet particles in hot gas stream. **(0.5 mark)**

**Principle of freeze drying:** Water is removed by **sublimation** (direct solid → vapor transition) under high vacuum, at temperatures below the triple point of water, thereby preserving heat-sensitive biomolecules. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Full-Length, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question B1-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**A continuous counter-current dialyzer is used to remove ammonium sulfate from a protein solution. The feed contains $C_0 = 0.50$ kg/m³ of ammonium sulfate, with feed flow rate $V_f = 0.020$ m³/min, membrane area $A = 2.5$ m², dialysis coefficient $K = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the steady-state solute concentration in the feed as a function of time for a batch dialyzer. State the assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the time required to remove **95%** of the initial salt from a 50 L feed batch. Also calculate the **residual concentration** at $t = 6$ hours.

---

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION (B1-A)

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

Assumptions:
1. Constant dialysis coefficient $K$ (membrane unchanged). **[1 mark]**
2. Well-mixed feed compartment (no internal concentration gradient). **[1 mark]**
3. Continuous dialysate replacement so $C_d \approx 0$. **[1 mark]**
4. Isothermal, no chemical reaction, no osmotic pressure effect. **[1 mark]**

Derivation:

**Step 1:** Solute flux through membrane by Fick's law:
$$N_s = K (C_f - C_d)$$

**Step 2:** With $C_d \approx 0$ and feed volume $V_f$:
$$N_s = K \cdot C_f$$

**Step 3:** Mass balance on the feed:
$$-V_f \frac{dC_f}{dt} = K A C_f$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables:
$$\frac{dC_f}{C_f} = -\frac{K A}{V_f} dt$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from $C_0$ to $C(t)$:
$$C(t) = C_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{K A}{V_f} t\right) \quad \text{[1 mark for final form]}$$

**Step 6:** State physical meaning: first-order decay; rate constant = $K A / V_f$. **[1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — 95% removal time:**
For 95% removal, $C(t)/C_0 = 0.05$. **[1 mark]**

$$0.05 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{K A}{V_f} t\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log:**
$$\ln(0.05) = -\frac{K A}{V_f}\, t$$

$$\frac{K A}{V_f} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(2.5)}{0.050} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Note: 50 L = 0.050 m³.

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{9.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.996}{9.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 3329 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**
$$t = 3329 / 3600 = 0.925 \text{ h} \approx 55.5 \text{ minutes} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4 — Residual concentration at 6 h:**
$$t = 6 \text{ h} = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \text{ s}$$

$$C(21600) = 0.50 \cdot \exp(-9.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 21{,}600)$$

$$C(21600) = 0.50 \cdot \exp(-19.44)$$

$$\exp(-19.44) = 3.65 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\boxed{C(6\text{ h}) = 1.82 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx 0} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** Beyond ~3 hours, additional dialysis provides negligible benefit (diminishing returns). This explains why industrial dialysis is typically limited to **3–4 effective half-lives**. **[1 mark]**

---

### ⭐ Question B1-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a) [7 marks]** A cooling crystallizer is charged with 200 L of a hot solution containing 0.45 kg/L of an organic acid. The solution is cooled to a temperature where the solubility of the acid is 0.10 kg/L. Assuming no evaporation, calculate the mass of crystals obtained and the **percent yield**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **McCabe ΔL Law** for crystal growth. How does it differ from the **delta-L law for nucleation**? Use a labeled diagram (flow description) to illustrate the population balance in a Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal (MSMPR) crystallizer.

---

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION (B1-B)

**Part (a) — Crystal Yield Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values:** **[1 mark]**
- $V_0 = 200$ L
- $C_0 = 0.45$ kg/L
- $C^* = 0.10$ kg/L
- No evaporation: $V_f = V_0 = 200$ L

**Step 2 — Apply mass balance:** **[1 mark]**
$$M_p = C_0 V_0 - C^* V_f$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:** **[1 mark]**
$$M_p = (0.45)(200) - (0.10)(200)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:** **[1 mark]**
$$M_p = 90 - 20 = 70 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Percent yield:** **[1 mark]**
$$Y = \frac{M_p}{C_0 V_0} \times 100\% = \frac{70}{90} \times 100\% = 77.78\%$$

**Step 6 — Comment on operating strategy:** Cooling crystallization alone gives ~78% yield; evaporative crystallization could push this to >90% but adds energy cost. **[1 mark]**

**Step 7 — Final boxed answer:** $\boxed{M_p = 70 \text{ kg}, \; Y = 77.78\%}$ **[1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) — McCabe ΔL Law and Population Balance [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Statement of McCabe's ΔL Law:** **[2 marks]**
*The growth rate $G$ of a crystal is independent of its size $L$.* All crystals in the same environment grow at the same linear rate (in m/s). Therefore, the time $\Delta t$ for a crystal to grow from size $L_1$ to $L_2$ is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{L_2 - L_1}{G}$$

**Step 2 — Consequence for CSD:** **[1 mark]**
The crystal size distribution **retains its shape** as it moves through the size axis. The mean size increases linearly with time.

**Step 3 — Comparison with Nucleation Kinetics:** **[1 mark]**
Nucleation **creates new crystals at the lowest size class ($L \approx 0$)**; growth **shifts all crystals to larger size classes at rate $G$**. These are two **distinct kinetic events** governed by different power-law rate equations.

**Step 4 — MSMPR Population Balance Equation (verbal + symbolic):** **[2 marks]**
For a well-mixed crystallizer with mean residence time $\tau$ and feed (clear liquor, no crystals):

$$G \frac{dn}{dL} + \frac{n}{\tau} = 0$$

with solution $n(L) = n_0 \exp(-L/G\tau)$. This is the **exponential size distribution** of crystals in MSMPR.

**Step 5 — Operating implication:** **[1 mark]**
- **Long residence time** → fewer, larger crystals.
- **Short residence time** → many small crystals.
- The design parameter is the **ratio $B/G$**, controlled by supersaturation and seeding.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls for Module 4:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in Dialysis:** Mixing seconds and hours; confusing $V_f$ (m³) with $L$; forgetting to convert when calculating $K A / V_f$.
> 2. **Yield Calculation Trap:** Adding the $\rho$-evaporation term to numerator and denominator inconsistently. Always **state your mass basis (kg solute vs. kg solution) explicitly**.
> 3. **Drying Rate Sign:** Forgetting that the rate is the **magnitude** of moisture loss; signs must be carried correctly in differential form.
> 4. **McCabe's ΔL Law ≠ "delta-L for nucleation"**: Many students confuse "ΔL" with a finite-difference (nucleation). It is the **growth law**; nucleation is a **birth term** in the population balance.
> 5. **Constant-Rate Period Assumption:** Many write the falling-rate equation when CRP still applies. **Always check** whether moisture content $X > X_c$ (CRP) or $X < X_c$ (FRP).

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Module 4 – Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Dialysis
- [x] **Membrane-based, diffusion-driven** separation; no phase change.
- [x] Driving force = **concentration gradient**; rate = first-order exponential decay.
- [x] $C(t) = C_0 \exp(-K A t / V_f)$ — **canonical KTU equation**.
- [x] **MWCO** is the membrane selection criterion (target protein MW > MWCO × 3 typically).
- [x] Industrial use: **desalting, buffer exchange** for proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids.
- [x] Hemodialysis (kidney machine) = the most famous industrial-medical application.

### Crystallization
- [x] **Liquid → solid** phase transition; governed by **supersaturation** ($\Delta C$).
- [x] Two kinetic events: **nucleation ($B$) and growth ($G$)**.
- [x] $B = k_b (\Delta C)^n$, $G = k_g (\Delta C)^m$.
- [x] **McCabe ΔL Law:** growth rate $G$ is **independent of crystal size $L$**.
- [x] Crystal yield (no evap.): $M_p = C_0 V_0 - C^* V_f$.
- [x] Crystal yield (with evap.): $M_p = (V_0 - V_f)\rho + C_0 V_0 - C^* V_f$.
- [x] Methods to induce supersaturation: **cooling, evaporation, anti-solvent addition, salting out, pH shift**.

### Drying
- [x] **Liquid → vapor** phase transition; usually water removal.
- [x] **Three periods:** initial adjustment, **constant rate period (CRP)**, **falling rate period (FRP)**.
- [x] CRP rate: $N_A = h_c (T_g - T_{wb}) / \lambda_w = k_y (Y_{wb} - Y_g)$.
- [x] FRP rate: governed by **internal moisture migration**; slower than CRP.
- [x] **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the transition point.
- [x] Common dryers: **tray, spray, fluidized-bed, drum, freeze (lyophilization)**.
- [x] **Freeze drying** = sublimation under high vacuum → best for heat-sensitive biologics (vaccines, antibodies).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts (Module 4)
- [x] All three operations are **mass transfer** processes; dialysis and drying are **non-phase-change** and **phase-change**, respectively, while crystallization is a **solid–liquid** phase change.
- [x] All three follow a **canonical pattern** in downstream processing: dialysis (purification) → crystallization (form change) → drying (stabilization).
- [x] Latent heat of vaporization of water = **2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C** (must memorize).
- [x] All time-dependent removal/production kinetics are first-order or power-law in nature.
- [x] **KTU numerical favorite:** dialysis time, crystal yield, drying rate calculations — all three are exam-favorite 7–14 mark problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Technologies in Downstream Processing in Biotechnology Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBTT601 - DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emerging Technologies in Downstream Processing in Biotechnology Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emerging Technologies in Downstream Processing (DSP)** refer to the next-generation separation, purification, and recovery technologies that overcome the limitations of conventional batch-wise chromatography and multi-step filtration. These technologies aim to achieve **higher recovery yields, reduced process time, lower capital expenditure (CAPEX), single-use flexibility, and integration with continuous manufacturing platforms** for biopharmaceutical, enzyme, and recombinant protein production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Framework**
> According to the PCBTT601 Module 4 syllabus, emerging DSP technologies encompass membrane-based separations, expanded bed adsorption, simulated moving bed chromatography, aqueous two-phase systems, supercritical fluid extraction, and continuous bioprocessing paradigms. Each of these technologies replaces one or more conventional unit operations with a more efficient, scalable, or cost-effective alternative.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a busy municipal water purification plant (representing the **crude fermentation broth**) that supplies drinking water (representing the **pure therapeutic protein**) to a city. Traditional DSP is like having **multiple separate filter tanks** where water is poured manually from one tank to the next — slow, wasteful, and prone to spillage.

Emerging DSP technologies are like installing a **fully automated, single integrated pipeline** where water flows continuously through smart membranes, adsorbers, and sensors — **no manual transfers, no batch waiting, and minimal product loss**. The result is faster delivery, better quality, and a smaller factory footprint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The conceptual understanding of *why* an emerging technology is "better" than a conventional one is worth at least **40%** of the marks in any 14-mark question. Examiners specifically look for your ability to compare features such as yield, purity, scale-up ease, and cost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Emerging DSP

The following performance parameters are universally applied to evaluate any emerging DSP technology and are **must-know** for KTU answers:

- **Yield (Recovery)** — expressed as a percentage (%)
- **Purity** — expressed as a percentage (%)
- **Fold Purification** — dimensionless ratio
- **Throughput / Productivity** — typically expressed in **g product $\cdot$ L$^{-1}$ $\cdot$ h$^{-1}$**
- **Host Cell Protein (HCP) removal** — expressed in **ppm** or **ng/mg**
- **Process Volume Reduction Factor (VRF)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** — expressed in **USD/g** or **INR/g**

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examiner Insight:** Always state the metric and its units when describing the performance of an emerging technology. Writing "high yield" without quantifying it loses marks. Write *"achieving a recovery yield of **>95%** with a process volume reduction of **10-fold**."*

### 1.4 Classification of Emerging DSP Technologies

For systematic study under the KTU framework, emerging DSP technologies can be classified into **five functional domains**:

1. **Solid-Liquid Separation Upgrades** — e.g., Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA), Acoustic Wave Separation
2. **Concentration & Capture Upgrades** — e.g., Membrane Chromatography, Simulated Moving Bed (SMB), Monoliths
3. **Liquid-Liquid Extraction Upgrades** — e.g., Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE), Ionic Liquid Extraction
4. **Cell Disruption Upgrades** — e.g., High-Pressure Homogenization, Bead Milling, PEF
5. **Process Integration Upgrades** — e.g., Continuous Manufacturing, Single-Use Systems, PAT-integrated DSP

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA)

**Operational Principle:** EBA eliminates the need for prior centrifugation or clarification. The feed broth is passed upward through a bed of adsorbent particles that are **classified by a downward flow of buffer**, creating a stable, expanded (fluidized) bed. Target product binds to the adsorbent; cells, debris, and particulates pass through.

**Why EBA Exists:** Conventional packed-bed chromatography fouls immediately when crude, particulate-laden broth is applied. EBA solves this by combining **clarification, concentration, and initial capture** in a single unit operation.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- The column is filled with adsorbent matrix (e.g., Streamline series).
- Feed broth enters from the **bottom** under controlled linear velocity.
- Bed expands to **2–3 times** its settled height.
- Particulates pass through unhindered (because of the void spaces in the fluidized bed).
- After loading, flow direction is **reversed** (top-down) to pack the bed.
- Elution is then performed as in normal packed-bed chromatography.

**Boundary Conditions:**
- Linear velocity must be greater than the **sedimentation velocity** of the matrix but lower than that of the cells.
- Typical expansion factor: **1.8 to 3.0**.
- pH, conductivity, and temperature must be tuned to favor binding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The phrase *"integrated capture"* is the single most important keyword examiners want when discussing EBA. Memorize that EBA integrates **clarification, concentration, and capture** in one step.

### 2.2 Membrane Chromatography

**Operational Principle:** The chromatographic ligand (e.g., Protein A, ion-exchange group, hydrophobic ligand) is covalently grafted onto the **internal surface of a microporous membrane** rather than onto the surface of spherical beads. The feed flows **convectively** through the membrane pores under pressure or vacuum.

**Why It Is Faster:** In bead-based chromatography, solutes travel by **slow diffusion** into the bead interior. In membrane chromatography, transport is by **convection** — orders of magnitude faster.

**KTU Formula Sheet — Critical Equations for Membrane Chromatography:**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | Volumetric flux through membrane (Darcy's Law) | $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| $R_m = \dfrac{r_p^2}{8 \cdot \tau \cdot \varepsilon}$ | Hydraulic resistance of membrane (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| $\text{Dyn. Capacity} = \dfrac{Q_{eq}}{V_m}$ | Dynamic binding capacity per membrane volume | $\text{g} \cdot \text{L}^{-1}$ |
| $\eta = \dfrac{J_{feed}}{J_{buffer}}$ | Membrane flux ratio (fouling indicator) | dimensionless |
| $\text{RR} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{C_{perm}}{C_{feed}}\right)$ | Rejection / removal ratio for impurities | dimensionless |

where:
- $\Delta P$ is the transmembrane pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of the feed (Pa·s)
- $r_p$ is the membrane pore radius (m)
- $\tau$ is the tortuosity (dimensionless)
- $\varepsilon$ is the membrane porosity (dimensionless)
- $Q_{eq}$ is the bound mass at equilibrium (g)
- $V_m$ is the membrane volume (L)
- $C_{perm}$ and $C_{feed}$ are permeate and feed concentrations

### 2.3 Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) Chromatography

**Operational Principle:** SMB mimics a true counter-current moving bed by using a **series of fixed columns** (typically 4–8 in number) with periodic shifting of inlet/outlet ports along the column train. This makes each zone appear to "move" in the opposite direction of the mobile phase.

**Key Zones in SMB:**
- **Zone I** — Desorbent regeneration
- **Zone II** — Separation of fast-moving component (extract)
- **Zone III** — Separation of slow-moving component (raffinate)
- **Zone IV** — Adsorbent regeneration

**Engineering Utility:** SMB is the gold standard for **enantiomeric separation** of chiral pharmaceuticals (e.g., separation of racemic mixtures into enantiomers for single-isomer drugs). It reduces solvent consumption by **up to 80%** compared to batch HPLC.

**Theoretical Plate Equivalent:** Although SMB uses a finite number of columns, the periodic port switching makes it equivalent to a column of **virtually infinite length**, yielding extremely high resolution.

### 2.4 Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE)

**Operational Principle:** When two polymers (e.g., PEG and dextran) or a polymer and a salt (e.g., PEG and phosphate) are mixed above critical concentrations, the solution **spontaneously separates into two immiscible aqueous phases**. Biomolecules partition differentially between the two phases based on hydrophobicity, charge, and molecular weight.

**Why It Is "Green":** Both phases are predominantly water (>70%), making ATPE **biocompatible, non-denaturing, and environmentally benign**.

**Partition Coefficient Formula (Critical for KTU):**

$$K = \frac{C_{top}}{C_{bottom}}$$

where $C_{top}$ and $C_{bottom}$ are the equilibrium concentrations of the target biomolecule in the top and bottom phases. A larger $K$ means better recovery in a single extraction.

### 2.5 Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)

**Operational Principle:** A supercritical fluid — most commonly **supercritical CO$_2$** ($T_c = 31.1^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 7.38$ MPa) — is used as the extraction solvent. It combines the **diffusivity of a gas** with the **solvating power of a liquid**.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** SFE is the industry standard for **decaffeination of coffee, hop extraction in brewing, and nutraceutical extraction** (e.g., omega-3 oils from fish, carotenoids from algae).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why supercritical CO$_2$ is preferred:** It is non-toxic, non-flammable, inexpensive, recyclable by depressurization, and operates at low temperatures that preserve heat-labile biomolecules.

### 2.6 Continuous Manufacturing & Single-Use Technologies

**Operational Principle:** Continuous DSP replaces batch-wise processing with **integrated, multi-column, real-time-controlled perfusion systems**. Single-use (disposable) systems use **pre-sterilized, plastic-based assemblies** that are discarded after each batch.

**Why It Matters for Industry:** The FDA (US) and EMA (Europe) actively endorse continuous manufacturing because it provides:
- Real-time release of product
- Smaller facility footprint (**~70% reduction**)
- Reduced water/buffer consumption (**~50% reduction**)
- Higher consistency (lower batch-to-batch variability)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since this module is **engineering-biology in nature** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivations" in Module 4 revolve around **quantitative design equations, performance comparisons, and analytical matrices**. The complete mathematical and logical derivations required for KTU are presented below.

### 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Binding Capacity in Membrane Chromatography

We will derive the **dynamic binding capacity (DBC)** at 10% breakthrough — the industry-standard measure for capture performance.

**Step 1 — Define the breakthrough curve.**

The breakthrough curve plots the **effluent concentration** ($C_{out}$) against the **loading volume** ($V$). At $V_{10\%}$, the effluent concentration reaches 10% of the feed concentration.

**Step 2 — Mass balance on the membrane.**

The mass of solute retained in the membrane at the breakthrough point is given by:

$$m_{bound} = \int_{0}^{V_{10\%}} \left( C_{in} - C_{out}(V) \right) dV$$

where $C_{in}$ is the inlet feed concentration. The integral represents the **area between the feed line and the breakthrough curve**, often calculated numerically via the trapezoidal rule.

**Step 3 — Normalize by membrane volume.**

The dynamic binding capacity at 10% breakthrough is then:

$$\text{DBC}_{10\%} = \frac{m_{bound}}{V_{membrane}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

A higher DBC means **more product can be captured per unit membrane volume** before saturation, directly reducing the membrane area required for a given batch.

**Step 5 — Typical numerical example (for KTU problem-solving).**

Suppose $C_{in} = 2.0$ g/L, $V_{10\%} = 12.5$ L, and $V_{membrane} = 0.5$ L. The integral area between the feed line and the breakthrough curve is approximately **22.5 g·min/L** (numerical integration). Therefore:

$$\text{DBC}_{10\%} = \frac{22.5 \text{ g}}{0.5 \text{ L}} = 45 \text{ g/L}$$

This matches typical industrial Protein A membrane capacities of **40–50 g/L**, confirming the validity of the calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Partition Coefficient in ATPE with Volume Ratio Correction

For a **single-stage ATPE**, the recovery yield in the top phase must account for both the partition coefficient $K$ and the volume ratio $R = V_{top}/V_{bottom}$.

**Step 1 — Define the recovery.**

The fraction of target biomolecule recovered in the top phase is:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{\text{mass in top phase}}{\text{total mass}} = \frac{C_{top} V_{top}}{C_{top} V_{top} + C_{bottom} V_{bottom}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the partition coefficient definition.**

Substituting $K = C_{top}/C_{bottom}$:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{K V_{top}}{K V_{top} + V_{bottom}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the volume ratio.**

Let $R = V_{top}/V_{bottom}$. Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_{bottom}$:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{K R}{K R + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

This formula is critically important because a high $K$ alone does **not** guarantee high recovery if $R$ is unfavorable. A large top-phase volume dilutes the target, reducing concentration even when total recovery is high. Process designers must optimize **both** $K$ and $R$ through tie-line selection on the phase diagram.

**Step 5 — Numerical verification.**

For $K = 4.0$ and $R = 0.5$:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{(4.0)(0.5)}{(4.0)(0.5) + 1} = \frac{2.0}{3.0} \approx 0.667 \text{ or } 66.7\%$$

If $R$ is increased to $2.0$:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{(4.0)(2.0)}{(4.0)(2.0) + 1} = \frac{8.0}{9.0} \approx 0.889 \text{ or } 88.9\%$$

This confirms that volume ratio strongly modulates recovery even at constant $K$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Evaluation of Emerging Technologies

The following matrix is the **single most important study aid** for KTU Module 4. Examiners frequently ask comparative 14-mark questions, and this matrix covers the exact dimensions they expect.

| Technology | Key Mechanism | Target Application | Recovery Range | Main Advantage | Main Limitation |
|------------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Expanded Bed Adsorption | Fluidized-bed capture | Crude feed clarification | **85–95%** | No pre-clarification needed | Bed stability critical |
| Membrane Chromatography | Convective binding | Capture / polishing | **80–95%** | 10$\times$ faster than bead columns | Lower capacity per volume |
| Simulated Moving Bed | Continuous counter-current | Chiral / enantiomer separation | **>95%** | Solvent savings up to **80%** | High complexity, modeling needed |
| Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction | Differential partitioning | Enzymes, plasmids | **70–90%** | Biocompatible, scalable | Polymer recovery is costly |
| Supercritical Fluid Extraction | SCF solvating power | Lipids, nutraceuticals | **80–95%** | Green, GRAS-grade CO$_2$ | High-pressure equipment |
| High-Pressure Homogenization | Shear + cavitation disruption | Intracellular products | **>90%** release | Continuous, scalable | Heat generation, fragile enzymes |
| Continuous Manufacturing | Perfusion + PAT | All biopharma | **>90%** | Reduced footprint & COGS | Process control complexity |
| Single-Use Systems | Disposable flow paths | All biopharma | Comparable to batch | Eliminates CIP/SIP | Plastic waste, extractables |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** When comparing in your answer, **always** state the recovery range, the main advantage, and the main limitation for each technology. Examiners award marks specifically for these three dimensions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conventional vs. Emerging DSP

| Process Step | Conventional Approach | Emerging Alternative | Key Improvement |
|--------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Cell removal | Centrifugation / depth filtration | Expanded bed adsorption / acoustic separation | One-step integrated capture |
| Initial capture | Packed-bed Protein A | Membrane chromatography | Convective flow → **10$\times$ faster** |
| Intermediate purification | Bead-based ion exchange | Monolith chromatography / SMB | Higher resolution at high flow |
| Final polishing | Bead-based RP / HIC | Membrane chromatography | Lower buffer consumption |
| Cell disruption | Bead mill / lysozyme | High-pressure homogenizer / PEF | Continuous, scalable |
| Solvent extraction | Organic solvent | ATPE / SCF-CO$_2$ | Biocompatible / green |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram: Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Crude Fermentation Broth]) --> nodePump[Feed Pump]
    nodePump --> nodeBottom[Bottom Inlet of Column]
    nodeBottom --> nodeExpand[Expanded Fluidized Bed]
    nodeExpand --> nodeBind[Target Product Binds to Matrix]
    nodeBind --> nodeCellsOut([Cells and Debris Pass Through Top])
    nodeBind --> nodeReverse[Reverse Flow Direction]
    nodeReverse --> nodePacked[Packed Bed Mode]
    nodePacked --> nodeElute[Elution Buffer Applied]
    nodeElute --> nodePure([Purified Product Eluate])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual learning tip:** Notice how EBA flips from upward flow (loading/expansion) to downward flow (elution). This dual-mode operation is what makes EBA unique and what examiners love to test.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Membrane Chromatography Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFeed([Crude Feed Reservoir]) --> nodeP[Tangential Pump]
    nodeP --> nodeMem[Membrane Cartridge with Grafted Ligands]
    nodeMem -- Target Binds --> nodeMem
    nodeMem --> nodeWash[Wash Buffer]
    nodeWash --> nodeElute[Elution Buffer]
    nodeElute --> nodeProduct([Pure Product Out])
    nodeMem --> nodeFT([Flow-Through Waste Stream])
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInlet1([Desorbent In]) --> nodeCol1[Column 1]
    nodeCol1 --> nodeCol2[Column 2]
    nodeCol2 --> nodeCol3[Column 3]
    nodeCol3 --> nodeCol4[Column 4]
    nodeCol4 --> nodeExtract([Extract Outlet - Fast Component])
    nodeCol3 --> nodeFeedIn([Feed Inlet])
    nodeCol4 --> nodeRaff([Raffinate Outlet - Slow Component])
    nodeSwitch[Port Switching Controller] -. Periodic Shift .-> nodeCol1
    nodeSwitch -. Periodic Shift .-> nodeCol2
    nodeSwitch -. Periodic Shift .-> nodeCol3
    nodeSwitch -. Periodic Shift .-> nodeCol4
```

### 4.4 Process Integration Flow: Continuous Manufacturing Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePerfusion[Perfusion Bioreactor] --> nodeCapture[Multi-Column Capture]
    nodeCapture --> nodeIntermediate[Intermediate Purification]
    nodeIntermediate --> nodePolish[Polishing Step]
    nodePolish --> nodeFormulation[Final Formulation]
    nodeFormulation --> nodeFill([Continuous Fill-Finish])
    nodePAT[Process Analytical Technology Sensors] -. Real-Time Monitoring .-> nodeCapture
    nodePAT -. Real-Time Monitoring .-> nodeIntermediate
    nodePAT -. Real-Time Monitoring .-> nodePolish
    nodeControl[Process Control System] --> nodeCapture
    nodeControl --> nodeIntermediate
    nodeControl --> nodePolish
```

### 4.5 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Emerging DSP Technologies

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Emerging DSP Technologies))
    Capture_Upgrade
      Expanded_Bed_Adsorption
      Membrane_Chromatography
      Monolith_Chromatography
    Separation_Upgrade
      Simulated_Moving_Bed
      Continuous_Chromatography
    Extraction_Upgrade
      Aqueous_Two_Phase
      Supercritical_Fluid
      Ionic_Liquid
    Disruption_Upgrade
      High_Pressure_Homogenizer
      Bead_Milling
      Pulsed_Electric_Field
    Process_Upgrade
      Single_Use_Systems
      Continuous_Manufacturing
      PAT_Integration
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA) and list any two advantages over conventional packed-bed chromatography.**

**Model Answer:**
Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA) is a chromatographic technique in which the adsorbent bed is fluidized by an upward flow of feed, allowing particulate-laden crude broth to be applied without prior clarification. The two main advantages are: (i) it integrates **clarification, concentration, and capture into a single unit operation**, and (ii) it eliminates the need for centrifugation or depth filtration upstream, significantly reducing process time and product loss.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2. What is supercritical fluid extraction (SFE)? Why is supercritical CO$_2$ preferred over other solvents for bioproduct recovery?**

**Model Answer:**
Supercritical fluid extraction (SFE) is a separation technique that uses a fluid above its critical temperature and critical pressure as the extraction solvent. Supercritical CO$_2$ is preferred because it is **non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe), inexpensive, easily recyclable by depressurization**, and operates at low temperatures (just above **31.1°C**) that preserve heat-labile biomolecules.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

#### **Question A (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**Q(a). With a neat diagram, explain the principle and operation of Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA) for the recovery of a recombinant protein from crude fermentation broth. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA) operates on the principle of **fluidized-bed chromatography**, where an upward flow of crude feed stabilizes the bed of adsorbent particles in an expanded state. The process consists of six sequential stages:

1. **Bed Preparation:** The column is filled with a specialized matrix (e.g., Streamline DEAE, Streamline SP) that has a defined density and size distribution enabling stable fluidization.
2. **Equilibration:** Upward flow of equilibration buffer (typically 2–3 bed volumes) expands the bed to a stable height, generally **2–3 times** the settled bed height.
3. **Loading:** Crude fermentation broth is applied upward. Target product binds to the ligands on the matrix; cells, debris, and particulates pass through the void spaces without clogging.
4. **Washing:** Upward flow of wash buffer removes loosely bound impurities and residual cells from the expanded bed.
5. **Elution:** The flow direction is **reversed** (top-down), packing the bed into normal packed-bed mode. Elution buffer is then applied to recover the bound product in a concentrated, purified form.
6. **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP):** Standard CIP protocols regenerate the matrix for the next cycle.

**Advantages:**
- Eliminates the need for separate centrifugation and clarification steps (**integrated capture**).
- Reduces total process time by up to **50–70%**.
- High recovery yields (**85–95%**) for intracellular and extracellular products.
- Scalable from laboratory to industrial scale without major redesign.

**Limitations:**
- Requires careful control of linear velocity for bed stability.
- Matrix cost is higher than conventional resins.
- Slightly lower binding capacity compared to packed-bed mode.
- Not suitable for very small feed volumes.

---

**Q(b). Describe the principle of Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) chromatography. Explain its advantages in enantiomer separation and discuss the role of the switching time interval. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) chromatography is a continuous separation technique that **simulates a true counter-current movement** between the solid adsorbent and the liquid mobile phase, using a finite number of fixed columns and periodic shifting of inlet/outlet ports.

**Principle:**
The system typically uses **4–8 columns** connected in series. There are four zones:
- **Zone I:** Located between the desorbent inlet and the extract outlet. The desorbent regenerates the solid adsorbent.
- **Zone II:** Located between the extract outlet and the feed inlet. The more strongly adsorbed component is recovered as the extract.
- **Zone III:** Located between the feed inlet and the raffinate outlet. The less strongly adsorbed component is recovered as the raffinate.
- **Zone IV:** Located between the raffinate outlet and the desorbent inlet. The solid is regenerated before re-entering Zone I.

**Switching Interval ($t_{sw}$):**
The switching time interval $t_{sw}$ determines how long each port configuration remains active before shifting by one column position. It is calculated as:

$$t_{sw} = \frac{L_{col}}{u_{solid}^{app}}$$

where $L_{col}$ is the length of a single column and $u_{solid}^{app}$ is the apparent solid velocity (equivalent to the shifting rate of the port train). If $t_{sw}$ is too short, the system does not reach equilibrium within a column; if too long, the components mix back, destroying separation.

**Advantages in Enantiomer Separation:**
- Continuous operation enables **higher productivity** than batch HPLC.
- Solvent consumption reduced by **up to 80%**.
- Product purity and yield both consistently exceed **99%** for well-tuned systems.
- Smaller column volumes required because each column is used efficiently.

**Limitations:**
- High initial capital investment.
- Requires sophisticated modeling (e.g., McCabe-Thiele, Triangle Theory).
- Not suitable when feed concentration varies widely.

#### **Question B (Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4]**

**Q(a). Explain the principle and operation of Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE) for the recovery of enzymes. Derive the recovery formula incorporating the partition coefficient and volume ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE) is a **liquid-liquid extraction** technique that uses two immiscible aqueous phases formed by mixing either two polymers (e.g., PEG–dextran) or a polymer and a salt (e.g., PEG–phosphate) above critical concentrations. Because both phases are predominantly water, the environment remains **biocompatible**, preserving enzyme activity and native structure.

**Principle:**
When the mixture settles, it separates into a **top phase** (typically PEG-rich, less polar) and a **bottom phase** (typically dextran- or salt-rich, more polar). Biomolecules partition between these phases based on hydrophobicity, charge, molecular weight, and surface properties.

**Operation:**
1. Crude feed is mixed with the phase-forming components at the desired composition.
2. The mixture is agitated to enhance mass transfer, then allowed to settle under gravity (or by centrifugation) for phase separation.
3. The two phases are separated physically. Target biomolecules are recovered from the preferred phase.
4. Phase components are recycled by ultrafiltration or back-extraction.

**Derivation of Recovery Formula:**
The recovery yield in the top phase is:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{K \cdot R}{K \cdot R + 1}$$

where $K = C_{top}/C_{bottom}$ is the partition coefficient and $R = V_{top}/V_{bottom}$ is the volume ratio.

**Derivation:**

$$Y_{top} = \frac{C_{top} V_{top}}{C_{top} V_{top} + C_{bottom} V_{bottom}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $C_{bottom} V_{bottom}$:

$$Y_{top} = \frac{(C_{top}/C_{bottom}) (V_{top}/V_{bottom})}{(C_{top}/C_{bottom}) (V_{top}/V_{bottom}) + 1} = \frac{K \cdot R}{K \cdot R + 1}$$

**Interpretation:** A high $K$ alone does not guarantee high recovery; the volume ratio $R$ must also be optimized via tie-line selection on the phase diagram.

---

**Q(b). Discuss the principle of membrane chromatography. Compare it with conventional bead-based chromatography in terms of mass transfer mechanism, flow rate, dynamic binding capacity, and processing time. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Membrane chromatography uses **microporous membranes with chromatographic ligands** (e.g., Protein A, ion-exchange groups, hydrophobic ligands) grafted onto the internal pore surfaces. The feed flows **convectively** through the membrane under pressure or vacuum, and target molecules bind to the ligands on the membrane walls.

**Mass Transfer Mechanism:**
- **Conventional bead-based chromatography:** Solutes are transported by **slow diffusion** into the interior of spherical beads. Diffusional path length scales with bead radius, leading to long residence times and broad peak shapes.
- **Membrane chromatography:** Solutes are transported by **convection** through the pores. The entire pore surface is accessible directly, eliminating intra-particle diffusion limitations.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Bead-Based Chromatography | Membrane Chromatography |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Mass transfer | Diffusive (slow) | Convective (fast) |
| Typical flow rate | 100–500 cm/h | 600–5000 cm/h |
| Dynamic binding capacity | 30–50 g/L (Protein A) | 20–40 g/L (Protein A) |
| Processing time (per cycle) | 30–60 minutes | 3–10 minutes |
| Buffer consumption | High | Reduced by up to **80%** |
| Scalability | Limited by pressure drop | Linear with membrane area |

**Conclusion:** Membrane chromatography trades a marginally lower binding capacity for a **10$\times$ faster processing time** and significantly reduced buffer consumption. It is ideal for **capture and polishing** steps where speed and throughput matter more than maximum binding density.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write generic statements like "membrane chromatography is faster" — quantify it (**"10$\times$ faster"**).
> - **Do NOT** confuse the direction of flow in EBA: loading is **upward**, elution is **downward**. Writing the reverse will lose 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** forget to derive the recovery formula in ATPE — examiners specifically look for the $Y_{top} = KR/(KR+1)$ equation with proper substitution steps.
> - **Do NOT** omit critical constants such as the critical temperature of CO$_2$ (**31.1°C**) in SFE questions — these are tested explicitly.
> - **Do NOT** mix up "extract" and "raffinate" ports in SMB questions — extract is the more strongly adsorbed component.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Emerging DSP Technologies**

- **Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA):** Fluidized bed that integrates clarification, concentration, and capture. Loading is upward, elution is downward. Recovery **85–95%**.
- **Membrane Chromatography:** Convective mass transfer via ligand-grafted microporous membranes. **10$\times$ faster** than bead-based, lower buffer usage, lower dynamic binding capacity.
- **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB):** Continuous counter-current separation using 4–8 columns with periodic port switching. Standard for **enantiomer separation**. Solvent savings **up to 80%**.
- **Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE):** Biocompatible PEG/dextran or PEG/salt systems. Recovery formula: $Y_{top} = KR/(KR+1)$. Suitable for **enzymes and plasmids**.
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE):** Uses supercritical CO$_2$ ($T_c = 31.1^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 7.38$ MPa). Green technology for lipids and nutraceuticals.
- **High-Pressure Homogenization:** Continuous cell disruption using shear + cavitation at pressures of **10,000–30,000 psi**.
- **Continuous Manufacturing:** Perfusion + multi-column capture + PAT. Reduces footprint by **~70%**, buffer use by **~50%**.
- **Single-Use Systems:** Pre-sterilized disposable flow paths. Eliminates CIP/SIP; concerns include extractables and plastic waste.
- **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF):** Emerging non-thermal cell disruption using short high-voltage pulses.
- **PAT (Process Analytical Technology):** Real-time sensors integrated into DSP for quality-by-design.
- **Comparison verbs to use in answers:** "in contrast," "whereas," "the key advantage lies in," "this is offset by," "a noteworthy limitation is."
- **Key formula memorization checklist:** Darcy's Law for membrane flux, partition coefficient $K$, recovery $Y_{top} = KR/(KR+1)$, breakthrough capacity $\text{DBC}_{10\%} = m_{bound}/V_{membrane}$.
- **Always quantify:** Never write "high yield" — write **">95%"**. Never write "faster" — write **"10$\times$ faster"**.
